headings.text: Start by Creating Two Lists: “Known” and “Unknown”
Background snapshot
1 — Set a timer for 10 minutes.
2 — Create two columns or headings: KNOWN | UNKNOWN.

3 — Under KNOWN, write 6 items you can state as facts or measurable observations. Use numbers where possible. Example: “I have $3,200 in checking,” “My commute is 34 minutes each way,” “I sleep 6 hours on weeknights (average),” “I have 3 design clients right now.”

4 — Under UNKNOWN, write everything you’re not sure about that matters to the decision. Avoid solutions for now. Example: “Will client A renew in 3 months?” “Can I shift to 7 hours of sleep with a 9–9.5 hour day?” “How much extra revenue if I raise price by 10%?”

5 — From UNKNOWN, pick the three highest‑leverage unknowns (the ones that would change your next month if resolved). For each, write one test or micro‑task that you can do within 48 hours. Make tests specific and time‑bounded: interviews, small experiments, measurements, or calls.
Types of unknowns
How to write a tight unknown
1 — Single objective: one question answered.
2 — Time cap: ≤90 minutes (prefer ≤30).
3 — Measure: one number to track (count, minutes, mg).
4 — Decision rule: what we will do if result is A, B, or C.
1 — Select one unknown that would change the next 30 days.
2 — Choose the method that matches the type (interview, landing page, dry run).
3 — Allocate 30 minutes in your calendar.
4 — Run the experiment and capture exactly one number or one short quote that matters.
5 — Decide next steps in the next 10 minutes: schedule the follow‑up, record a decision in Brali, or plan a second micro‑test.
Recommended working set sizes
Sample Day Tally (how to reach a target by applying this hack)
Sample Day Tally (day 1 activities)
Decision rule templates
Edge cases and limits
1 — Stop the snowball: limit unknowns to the working set (3–5) and write a “defer” list for everything else.
2 — Turn unknowns into time‑boxed experiments: 24–72 hours for small tests, 7–14 days for pilots.
3 — Quantify cost of being wrong: estimate the downside in dollars/hours to decide how much to invest in testing.
Script for sharing unknowns (90 seconds)
1 — State the knowns (2–3 quick facts).
2 — State the top 3 unknowns and why they matter (one sentence each).
3 — Propose one test per unknown and ask for volunteers for micro‑tasks.
Suggested weekly template (45 minutes)
1 — Review last week’s top 3 unknowns and their test results (10 min).
2 — Decide which unknowns to retire and which to keep (10 min).
3 — For each kept unknown, design one 30‑minute micro‑task for the coming week (15 min).
4 — Log tasks and check‑ins in Brali LifeOS (10 min).

1 — We have an email list of 1,200 people.
2 — Current open rate: 22% (average).
3 — Engagement: 120 people click on articles monthly.
4 — Content completion rate for free mini‑course: 28%.
5 — Our production capacity: 30 hours of video production in 4 weeks.
6 — We can spend $400 on paid outreach.

3 — Can we produce and quality‑check the content in 4 weeks? (Process)
4 — Is our 1,200 list reachable with a 20% conversion to launch interest? (Channel)
5 — Are there legal or licensing restrictions on templates? (System)

2 — Preference unknown test: Interview 10 paying customers about which template features they value (10 interviews × 15 min = 150 minutes). (Split across two days.)
3 — Process unknown test: Produce a 15‑minute pilot lesson and run it with 10 beta testers; measure content completion and feedback (3 hours to produce, 30 min feedback session).
4 — Channel unknown test: Send an email to the top 1,000 subscribers promoting the pilot lesson; track CTR and signups (30 min to craft).
5 — Legal unknown test: Ask an expert or check licenses for template content (60 minutes).

howTo.title: Create the Known and Unknown Lists
howTo.summary: Spend 10 minutes writing down 6 known facts and 6 unknowns related to your decision. Separate what you are certain about from what remains uncertain.

howTo.title: Identify High-Leverage Unknowns
howTo.summary: From your unknowns, select the top 3 that could significantly impact your next steps. For each, design a specific micro-task to investigate further.

howTo.title: Run the Micro-Tasks
howTo.summary: Execute the micro-tasks within 48 hours. Focus on gathering measurable evidence that will help clarify your unknowns and inform your decisions.

howTo.title: Review and Analyze Results
howTo.summary: After completing the micro-tasks, analyze the results to determine what actions to take next. Use the insights to refine your approach.

howTo.title: Check in with LifeOS Brali app
howTo.summary: Log your results and insights in the Brali LifeOS app to track progress and maintain accountability. Access it at https://metalhatscats.com/life-os/known-unknown-decision-lab/.
